---
title: "BKP: An R Package for Beta Kernel Process Modeling"
shorttitle: "BKP"
# date: "2026.04.11"
header-includes:
  - \AtBeginDocument{\author[Jiangyan Zhao (ECNU)]{Jiangyan Zhao}}
  - \AtBeginDocument{\date[2026.07.24 (Kunming)]{2026.07.24}}
  - \renewcommand*{\bibfont}{\footnotesize}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \graphicspath{{../code/figure/}}
institute: 
  - School of Statistics, ECNU
  - jyzhao@sfs.ecnu.edu.cn
  - joint work with Kunhai Qing and Jin Xu
latex_engine: pdflatex
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    slide_level: 3
    keep_tex: true
    keep_md: true
    citation_package: natbib
    includes:
      in_header: preamble.tex
bibliography: ../paper/refs.bib
biblio-style: "chicago"
link-citations: true
classoption: "aspectratio=169"
colorlinks: true    # 启用链接颜色
citecolor: blue     # 设置引文链接颜色（如 blue, red, green, magenta）
urlcolor: blue      # 设置 URL 链接颜色
linkcolor: red      # 设置内部跳转链接颜色（如目录）
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(kableExtra)

## Use the directory containing BKP_Slides.Rmd as the working directory
## for all R code chunks.
slides_dir <- dirname(
  normalizePath(
    knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE),
    winslash = "/"
  )
)

knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = slides_dir)
```

# Motivation


### Estimating an Unknown Probability Surface

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex2.pdf}
\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.5\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Observation model}
\[
  y_i \mid \pi(\vx_i) \sim \operatorname{Binomial} \!\left(m_i,\pi(\vx_i)\right).
\]


\item \textbf{Objective}

Estimate the underlying probability surface \(\pi(\vx)\) and quantify its pointwise uncertainty.
\end{itemize}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


### The Gap Between Flexibility and Tractable Inference

\begin{columns}[T,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.46\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Logistic regression}
$$
  \pi(\vx) = \operatorname{logit}^{-1} \!\left(\vx^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}\right)
$$

\begin{center}
\textcolor{red}{Fast and interpretable}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{blue}{Limited nonlinear flexibility}
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.46\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Gaussian process classification}
$$
  \pi(\vx)=\operatorname{logit}^{-1}\{f(\vx)\}, \quad f(\vx)\sim\operatorname{GP}
$$

\begin{center}
\textcolor{red}{Flexible probability surfaces}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{blue}{Approximate posterior inference}\\[-0.15em]
\scriptsize
\citep{Rasmussen2006GPML,Nickisch2008BinaryGP}
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.8em}

\begin{alertblock}{Motivation}
Can we retain nonlinear flexibility while performing direct, closed-form updating on the probability scale?
\end{alertblock}


# Methodology

### Beta Kernel Process: Direct Probability-Scale Updating

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]
\hspace*{-0.02\textwidth}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Prior
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.28\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Beta prior}
\footnotesize
$$
  \pi(\vx) \sim \operatorname{Beta} \bigl\{\alpha_0(\vx),\beta_0(\vx) \bigr\}.
$$

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{center}
Prior information on the probability scale
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.05\textwidth}
\centering
\Large
\[
+
\]
\end{column}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Local evidence
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.32\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Local likelihood}
\footnotesize
$$
\begin{aligned}
  k_i(\vx) &= k(\vx,\vx_i;\vtheta),\\[0.4em]
  S(\vx) &= \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i(\vx)y_i,\\[0.4em]
  F(\vx) &= \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i(\vx)(m_i-y_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{center}
  Nearby observations receive greater weight
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.05\textwidth}
\centering
\Large
\[
\Rightarrow
\]
\end{column}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Posterior
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.33\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Beta posterior}

\footnotesize

$$
\begin{aligned}
  \pi(\vx)\mid\calD &\sim \operatorname{Beta} \bigl\{\alpha_n(\vx),\beta_n(\vx)\bigr\},\\[0.4em]
  \alpha_n(\vx) &= \alpha_0(\vx)+S(\vx),\\[0.4em]
  \beta_n(\vx) &= \beta_0(\vx)+F(\vx).
\end{aligned}
$$

\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{center}
Closed-form posterior inference
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{alertblock}{Key idea}
\centering
\textbf{Local kernel weighting}
\quad \(+\) \quad
\textbf{Beta--binomial conjugacy} \\
\(\Longrightarrow\) \quad
\textbf{flexible modeling with closed-form posterior inference}.
\end{alertblock}


\vfill
\footnotesize
BKP is a \textcolor{red}{\bf Bayesian-inspired} probability-scale smoother based on kernel-weighted local-likelihood conjugate updating
\citep{Goetschalckx:2011,MacKenzie2014,Rolland2019BKP,Fan1998LocalMLE}.


### Closed-Form Posterior Summaries

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]
\begin{column}{0.35\textwidth}
\begin{block}{Probability estimation}
\footnotesize
$$
\widehat{\pi}(\vx)=\frac{\alpha_n(\vx)}{\alpha_n(\vx)+\beta_n(\vx)}
$$

\begin{center}
Posterior mean of the unknown probability surface
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.6\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Pointwise uncertainty}
\footnotesize
$$
\operatorname{Var}\{\pi(\vx)\mid\calD\}=
\frac{\widehat{\pi}(\vx)\{1-\widehat{\pi}(\vx)\}}
{\alpha_n(\vx)+\beta_n(\vx)+1}
$$
$$
\operatorname{CI}_{1-\delta}(\vx)=
\left[Q_{\delta/2}(\vx),Q_{1-\delta/2}(\vx)\right]
$$

\begin{center}
Credible intervals follow from Beta posterior quantiles
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.2em}
\begin{block}{Count prediction}
\small
$$
Y_{\mathrm{new}}\mid\calD,\vx,m_{\mathrm{new}}
\sim
\operatorname{Beta\text{-}Binomial}
\left\{m_{\mathrm{new}},\alpha_n(\vx),\beta_n(\vx)\right\}.
$$

\begin{center}
Closed-form prediction for future binomial counts
\end{center}

\end{block}


\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{alertblock}{Computational implication}
\centering
Conditional on the kernel hyperparameters, posterior summaries and predictive distributions are available without numerical posterior approximation.
\end{alertblock}

### Effective-Sample-Size Calibration

\begin{alertblock}{Idea}
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item When trial sizes are {\bf heterogeneous}, the standard kernel-weighted data contribution to \textcolor{red}{posterior precision},
$$
  m_K(\vx)=\vk(\vx)^\top\vm,
$$
may {\bf misrepresent} the intended amount of local information.
\item ESS calibration {\bf rescales} the data pseudo-count to a Shepard-based target \citep{Shepard1968} while preserving the kernel-weighted \textcolor{red}{empirical success proportion}.
\end{itemize}
\end{alertblock}

\vspace{-1em}
\begin{columns}[T,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Target ESS}
\footnotesize

$$\begin{aligned}
  m_{\mathrm{tar}}(\vx) & = \rho(\vx)m_{\mathrm S}(\vx), \\
  \rho(\vx) & = \max_{1\le i\le n} k(\vx,\vx_i).
\end{aligned}$$


\(m_{\mathrm S}(\vx)\) \textcolor{red}{interpolates} the observed trial sizes.

\vspace{0.3em}

\(\rho(\vx)\) \textcolor{blue}{attenuates} the target ESS in regions with weak data support.


\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.49\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Calibrated update}
\footnotesize

For \(m_K(\vx)>0\), define $c(\vx) = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{tar}}(\vx)}{m_K(\vx)}$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_n^{\mathrm{ESS}}(\vx)
&=
\alpha_0(\vx)
+
c(\vx)\vk(\vx)^\top\vy,\\[0.4em]
\beta_n^{\mathrm{ESS}}(\vx)
&=
\beta_0(\vx)
+
c(\vx)\vk(\vx)^\top(\vm-\vy).
\end{aligned}
$$

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

### Prior Specification

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.4\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Non-informative prior}
\small
\[
\pi(\vx)\sim\operatorname{Beta}(1,1).
\]

\begin{center}
Default uniform prior when no external information is available
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Informative prior with fixed mean}
\small
$$
\alpha_0(\vx)=r_0p_0, \quad \beta_0(\vx)=r_0(1-p_0)
$$
$$
\mathbb{E}\{\pi(\vx)\}=p_0, \quad \alpha_0(\vx)+\beta_0(\vx)=r_0
$$

\begin{center}
A fixed \textcolor{red}{prior mean} \(p_0\) with \textcolor{blue}{precision} controlled by \(r_0\)
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.2em}
\begin{block}{Data-adaptive prior}
\small
$$
p(\vx)= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{k(\vx,\vx_i)}{\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}k(\vx,\vx_i)}\frac{y_i}{m_i}
, \quad r(\vx)=r_0\sum_{i=1}^{n}k(\vx,\vx_i)
$$
$$
\alpha_0(\vx)=r(\vx)p(\vx), \quad
\beta_0(\vx)=r(\vx)\{1-p(\vx)\}.
$$

\begin{center}
The \textcolor{red}{prior mean} follows the local empirical proportion, while the \textcolor{blue}{prior precision} adapts to the local kernel mass
\end{center}

\end{block}


<!-- ### Scaled Distance and Length-Scale Parameters -->

<!-- \begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth] -->

<!-- \begin{column}{0.48\textwidth} -->

<!-- \begin{block}{Scaled distance} -->
<!-- \small -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!--   h(\vx,\vx';\vtheta)=\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\frac{x_j-x_j'}{\theta_j}\right)^2}. -->
<!-- $$ -->

<!-- \begin{itemize} -->
<!-- \item Smaller \(\theta_j\): more localized borrowing -->
<!-- \item Larger \(\theta_j\): broader borrowing -->
<!-- \end{itemize} -->

<!-- \end{block} -->

<!-- \end{column} -->

<!-- \hfill -->

<!-- \begin{column}{0.47\textwidth} -->

<!-- \begin{block}{Length-scale structure} -->
<!-- \small -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!-- \text{Isotropic:}\qquad -->
<!-- \theta_1=\cdots=\theta_d=\theta -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!-- \text{Anisotropic:}\qquad -->
<!-- \vtheta=(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_d) -->
<!-- $$ -->

<!-- \begin{center} -->
<!-- An isotropic kernel uses a common length scale across dimensions, whereas an anisotropic kernel allows dimension-specific borrowing ranges -->
<!-- \end{center} -->

<!-- \end{block} -->

<!-- \end{column} -->

<!-- \end{columns} -->

<!-- \vspace{0.5em} -->

<!-- \begin{alertblock}{Modeling role} -->
<!-- \centering -->
<!-- The length-scale parameters determine how rapidly information borrowing decreases with distance. -->
<!-- \end{alertblock} -->


<!-- ### Kernel Functions -->

<!-- \begin{block}{Implemented kernels} -->
<!-- \small -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!-- \begin{aligned} -->
<!-- \text{Gaussian:}\qquad -->
<!-- k(h) &= \exp(-h^2),\\[0.5em] -->
<!-- \text{Mat{\'e}rn }5/2:\qquad -->
<!-- k(h) &= \left(1+\sqrt{5}h+\frac{5}{3}h^2\right)\exp(-\sqrt{5}h),\\[0.5em] -->
<!-- \text{Mat{\'e}rn }3/2:\qquad -->
<!-- k(h) &= \left(1+\sqrt{3}h\right)\exp(-\sqrt{3}h),\\[0.5em] -->
<!-- \text{Wendland:}\qquad -->
<!-- k(h) &= (\zeta h+1)\max\{0,1-h\}^{\zeta}, \quad \zeta=\lfloor d/2\rfloor+3. -->
<!-- \end{aligned} -->
<!-- $$ -->
<!-- \end{block} -->

<!-- \vspace{0.4em} -->

<!-- \begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth] -->

<!-- \begin{column}{0.47\textwidth} -->
<!-- \begin{block}{Gaussian and Mat{\'e}rn} -->
<!-- \centering -->
<!-- Noncompact support -->

<!-- Every observation at a finite distance receives a positive weight, although the weight decreases with distance. -->
<!-- \end{block} -->
<!-- \end{column} -->

<!-- \hfill -->

<!-- \begin{column}{0.47\textwidth} -->
<!-- \begin{block}{Wendland} -->
<!-- \centering -->
<!-- Compact support -->

<!-- Observations with \(h\geq 1\) receive exactly zero weight. -->
<!-- \end{block} -->
<!-- \end{column} -->

<!-- \end{columns} -->


### Kernel Functions and Length Scales

:::::: {.cols data-latex=""}

::: {.col data-latex="{0.4\textwidth}"}

\begin{block}{Scaled distance}
\small
$$
  h(\vx,\vx';\vtheta) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\frac{x_j-x_j'}{\theta_j}\right)^2}.
$$
\end{block}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{block}{Length-scale structure}
\centering
\small
Isotropic: $\theta_1=\cdots=\theta_d=\theta$ 

\vspace{0.3em}

Anisotropic: $\vtheta=(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_d)$
\end{block}

:::

::: {.col data-latex="{0.05\textwidth}"}
\ 
:::

::: {.col data-latex="{0.55\textwidth}"}

```{r kernel-shape-figure, echo=FALSE, fig.width=4.5, fig.height=3, out.width='100%'}
library(ggplot2)

h <- seq(0, 3, length.out = 500)

## Wendland kernel is illustrated for d = 2
d_plot <- 2
zeta <- floor(d_plot / 2) + 3

kernel_df <- rbind(
  data.frame(
    h = h,
    weight = exp(-h^2),
    kernel = "Gaussian"
  ),
  data.frame(
    h = h,
    weight = (1 + sqrt(5) * h + 5 * h^2 / 3) * exp(-sqrt(5) * h),
    kernel = "Matérn 5/2"
  ),
  data.frame(
    h = h,
    weight = (1 + sqrt(3) * h) * exp(-sqrt(3) * h),
    kernel = "Matérn 3/2"
  ),
  data.frame(
    h = h,
    weight = (zeta * h + 1) * pmax(0, 1 - h)^zeta,
    kernel = "Wendland"
  )
)

kernel_df$kernel <- factor(
  kernel_df$kernel,
  levels = c(
    "Gaussian",
    "Matérn 5/2",
    "Matérn 3/2",
    "Wendland"
  )
)

ggplot(
  kernel_df,
  aes(
    x = h,
    y = weight,
    color = kernel,
    linetype = kernel
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.05) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 1,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 1.08,
    y = 0.08,
    label = "Wendland cutoff",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 2.05,
    y = 0.16,
    label = "Noncompact support",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 3),
    breaks = 0:3,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1.02),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.25),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Scaled distance h",
    y = "Kernel weight k(h)",
    color = NULL,
    linetype = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(4, 4, 2, 4)
  )
```

:::

::::::


\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{alertblock}{Borrowing range}
\centering
\small
The kernel family controls the shape of information decay,
whereas \(\vtheta\) controls the borrowing range.
\end{alertblock}


### LOOCV-Based Length-Scale Selection

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Leave-one-out quantities}
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item \(\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)\): predicted probability at \(\vx_i\) after excluding observation \(i\).
\item \(\widetilde{\pi}_i=y_i/m_i\): observed empirical proportion.
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.41\textwidth}

\begin{alertblock}{Selection criterion}
\small
$$
\widehat{\vtheta}
=
\underset{\vtheta}{\argmin}\,
L(\vtheta;\calD_n),
\quad
L\in\{\mathrm{BS},\mathrm{LL}\}.
$$
\end{alertblock}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{block}{Brier score}
\small
$$
\mathrm{BS}(\vtheta)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left\{\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)-\widetilde{\pi}_i\right\}^{2}.
$$
\end{block}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{block}{Log-loss}
\small
$$
\mathrm{LL}(\vtheta)=-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left[
\widetilde{\pi}_i\log\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)
+(1-\widetilde{\pi}_i)\log\{1-\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)\}
\right].
$$
\end{block}

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
All leave-one-out predictions are obtained from one kernel matrix by setting its diagonal entries to zero.
\end{center}


### Multi-Start Hyperparameter Optimization

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.42\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Log-scale reparameterization}
\small
$$
\gamma_j=\log_{10}(\theta_j),
\quad
\theta_j=10^{\gamma_j}.
$$
$$
d_\gamma=
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{isotropic},\\
d, & \text{anisotropic}.
\end{cases}
$$

\begin{center}
The transformation enforces positive length-scale parameters
\end{center}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.53\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Optimization workflow}
\small
\begin{enumerate}

  \item Generate \(n_0=10d_\gamma\) initial values using a Latin hypercube design

  \item Run the derivative-free SBPLX algorithm from each initial value

  \item Retain the solution with the smallest LOOCV loss

  \end{enumerate}
$$
  s^\star= \underset{1\leq s\leq n_0}{\argmin}\; L\!\left(\widehat{\vgamma}^{(s)};\calD_n\right),
\quad \widehat{\vgamma} = \widehat{\vgamma}^{(s^\star)}.
$$

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{alertblock}{Why multi-start?}
\centering
The LOOCV objective may be non-convex, so multiple initial values reduce sensitivity to a single local optimum.
\end{alertblock}


# Package Usage

### \fct{fit\_BKP} --- Data Inputs

```{r fit-BKP, eval=FALSE}
fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = NULL,
  prior = "noninformative", r0 = 2, p0 = mean(y / m),
  kernel = "gaussian", isotropic = TRUE, theta = NULL,
  loss = "brier", ess = "none", n_multi_start = NULL, n_threads = 1)
```

- The first group specifies the core **data inputs**:

  - `X`: an \(n\times d\) numeric input matrix or data frame
  
  - `y`: a length-\(n\) vector of observed success counts
  
  - `m`: a length-\(n\) vector of corresponding binomial trial counts
  
  - `Xbounds`: an optional \(d\times2\) matrix of lower and upper bounds, used to normalize `X` to \([0,1]^d\) before fitting. If `NULL`, `X` is assumed to be already normalized.


### \fct{fit\_BKP} --- Prior Specification

```{r fit-BKP-prior, eval=FALSE}
fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = NULL,
  prior = "noninformative", r0 = 2, p0 = mean(y / m),
  kernel = "gaussian", isotropic = TRUE, theta = NULL,
  loss = "brier", ess = "none", n_multi_start = NULL, n_threads = 1)
```

- The second group specifies the **prior distribution**:

  - `prior`: selects `"noninformative"` (default), `"fixed"`, or `"adaptive"`
  
  - the prior is constructed using the \fct{get\_prior} function
  
  - `r0` and `p0`: global prior precision and mean


### \fct{fit\_BKP} --- Kernel Specification

```{r fit-BKP-kernel, eval=FALSE}
fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = NULL,
  prior = "noninformative", r0 = 2, p0 = mean(y / m),
  kernel = "gaussian", isotropic = TRUE, theta = NULL,
  loss = "brier", ess = "none", n_multi_start = NULL, n_threads = 1)
```

- The third group specifies the **kernel and length-scale structure**:

  - `kernel`: `"gaussian"` (default), `"matern52"`, `"matern32"`, or `"wendland"`
  
  - `isotropic`: `TRUE` uses one shared length scale; `FALSE` uses dimension-specific length scales
  
  - `theta`: a positive scalar for isotropic kernels, or a scalar/length-\(d\) vector for anisotropic kernels
  
  - If `theta = NULL`, it is selected by LOOCV; otherwise, the supplied value is used and optimization is skipped


### \fct{fit\_BKP} --- Tuning and Computation

```{r fit-BKP-tuning, eval=FALSE}
fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = NULL,
  prior = "noninformative", r0 = 2, p0 = mean(y / m),
  kernel = "gaussian", isotropic = TRUE, theta = NULL,
  loss = "brier", ess = "none", n_multi_start = NULL, n_threads = 1)
```

- The final group controls **hyperparameter tuning, ESS calibration, and computation**:

  - `loss`: `"brier"` (default) or `"log_loss"` for LOOCV tuning
  
  - `ess`: `"none"` uses the standard BKP update; `"shepard"` calibrates the kernel-weighted data precision
  
  - `n_multi_start`: number of optimization starts; if `NULL`, the default is \(10\) for isotropic kernels and \(10d\) for anisotropic kernels
  
  - `n_threads`: number of threads used in multi-start optimization; relevant only when `theta = NULL`
  

### \fct{fit\_BKP} --- Returned Object

```{r fit-BKP-object, eval=FALSE}
BKPmodel <- fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = Xbounds)
```

- The returned `BKP` object stores:

  - the fitted kernel, length-scale parameters, and LOOCV results

  - the prior specification and normalized training data

  - the prior parameters \(\alpha_0\) and \(\beta_0\)

  - the posterior parameters \(\alpha_n\) and \(\beta_n\)

- The fitted object supports standard S3 methods, including \fct{summary}, \fct{predict}, \fct{plot}, \fct{simulate}, and \fct{print}.


# Examples and Applications

## Synthetic Examples

### Logistic Probability Curve

- Let \(x\in[-2,2]\), and define the true success-probability function as
  $$
	  \pi_{1}(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-3x}}.
  $$
  
- We generate binomial observations at \(n=7\) input locations.

- This monotone logistic curve provides a simple benchmark for demonstrating the standard BKP workflow.

- \href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper/blob/master/code/s4_ex1_bkp_1d_logistic.R}{Reproduction code}


### Model Fitting

```{r, include=FALSE}
set.seed(123)
library(BKP)
library(tgp)
```

```{r}
# Define true success probability function
true_pi_fun <- function(x) {
  1/(1+exp(-3*x))
}

# Dataset
n <- 7 
Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2,2), nrow = 1)
X <- lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds)
true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X)
m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)
y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)

# Fit BKP model
BKP_model_1D_1 <- fit_BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = Xbounds)
```

### Fitted Model Summary

\small
```{r example1-summary}
summary(BKP_model_1D_1)
```

### Prediction at New Inputs

```{r}
Xnew <- lhs(n = 10, rect = Xbounds)
predict(BKP_model_1D_1, Xnew)
```

### Posterior Inference and Simulation

```{r, eval=FALSE}
plot(BKP_model_1D_1)
Xnew <- matrix(seq(-2, 2, length = 100), ncol = 1)
sim <- simulate(BKP_model_1D_1, nsim = 3, Xnew = Xnew)
```

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.45\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex1.pdf}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.45\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex1_sim.pdf}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


### Highly Nonlinear Probability Curve

- Unlike the monotone logistic curve in Example 1, this probability function is non-monotone and locally oscillatory.

- For \(x\in[-2,2]\), define
  $$
	  \pi_{2}(x) = \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1+e^{-x^{2}}\cos \left( 10\frac{1-e^{-x}}{1+e^{-x}} \right) \right],
  $$

- We generate \(n=30\) binomial observations and fit the default BKP model.

- \href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper/blob/master/code/s4_ex2_bkp_1d_nonlinear.R}{\textcolor{blue}{Reproduction code}}

### Posterior Inference for the Nonlinear Curve

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.5\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex2.pdf}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.5\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex2_sim.pdf}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace*{-1em}

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
\href{https://jiangyan-zhao.github.io/BKP-website}
{\textcolor{blue}{Explore the interactive example}}
\end{center}

### Effect of ESS Calibration on Pointwise Intervals

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]
\hspace*{-1em}
\begin{column}{0.5\textwidth}
  \vspace*{-10em}
  \resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%
	\centering
	\begin{tabular}{lllc}
		\toprule
		Evaluation set & Sample size & Model & Coverage (\%) \\
		\midrule
		\multirow{4}{*}{Evaluation grid}
		& \multirow{2}{*}{$n=30$}
		& BKP     & 87.1 (6.4) \\
		& & BKP-ESS & 94.1 (3.1) \\
		\cmidrule{2-4}
		& \multirow{2}{*}{$n=100$}
		& BKP     & 90.9 (4.1) \\
		& & BKP-ESS & 97.6 (1.0) \\
		\midrule
		\multirow{4}{*}{Training locations}
		& \multirow{2}{*}{$n=30$}
		& BKP     & 88.1 (6.7) \\
		& & BKP-ESS & 94.6 (3.3) \\
		\cmidrule{2-4}
		& \multirow{2}{*}{$n=100$}
		& BKP     & 90.8 (4.1) \\
		& & BKP-ESS & 97.6 (1.0) \\
		\bottomrule
	\end{tabular}
  }
\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex2_combined.pdf}
\end{column}

\end{columns}


### Two-Dimensional Probability Surface

- Let \(\vx\in[0,1]^2\).

- A nonlinear latent surface \(f(\vx)\) is constructed from a rescaled Goldstein--Price function \citep{Picheny2013Benchmark}.

- The true success probability is
  \[
    \pi(\vx)=\Phi\{f(\vx)\},
  \]
  where \(\Phi(\cdot)\) is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

- We generate \(n=100\) binomial observations and fit the BKP model.

- \href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper/blob/master/code/s4_ex3_bkp_2d_goldstein_price.R}{\textcolor{blue}{Reproduction code}}

### Posterior Inference for the Two-Dimensional Surface

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.32\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex3_true.pdf}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.6\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex3.pdf}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

### Comparison of Computation Times

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{elapsed_time.pdf}


## Real-Data Application

### Mourning Warbler Distribution Modeling

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.57\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mourning_warbler_map_distribution.pdf}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.45\textwidth}

\begin{itemize}
\small
\item Presence--absence data from the North American Breeding Bird Survey \citep{sauer2014north}

\item \(1{,}613\) training observations and \(301\) spatially withheld test observations

\item Eight continuous bioclimatic predictors

\item Longitude and latitude are used only for spatial partitioning and visualization

\item The response is Bernoulli, so \(m_i=1\) for every observation
\end{itemize}
\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{center}
\href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper/blob/master/code/s5_app2_mourning_warbler_sdm.R}{\textcolor{blue}{Reproduction code}}
\end{center}

### Predicted Presence Probability and Posterior Uncertainty

\begin{center}
\hspace*{-0.1\textwidth}
\includegraphics[width=1.2\linewidth]{mourning_warbler_map_bkp.pdf}
\end{center}


# Extensions

## TwinBKP

### Scalable BKP via a Global--Local Approximation

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.35\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Full BKP}
\small

Full BKP uses all \(n\) observations at every input location.
\begin{itemize}
\item Tuning cost: $\mathcal{O}(Tn^2)$
\item Memory: $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$
%\item Pointwise prediction: $\mathcal{O}(n)$
\end{itemize}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.6\textwidth}
\begin{alertblock}{Global--local idea}
\centering
\begin{itemize}
\item The global subset provides broad coverage of the input space.
\item Prediction-specific local neighbours refine the posterior near the target location.
\item ref: \citet{Vakayil2022Twinning,Vakayil2024TwinGP}
\end{itemize}
\end{alertblock}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


\begin{block}{TwinBKP subsets}
\small

A representative global subset is shared across all prediction locations:
$$
  G\subset\{1,\ldots,n\}, \qquad |G|=g.
$$

For each target \(\vx\), select \(\ell\) nearest non-global observations:
$$
  L(\vx)\subset\{1,\ldots,n\}\setminus G, \qquad |L(\vx)|=\ell.
$$

% The prediction-specific subset is $I(\vx)=G\cup L(\vx)$.

\end{block}


### Two-Stage Conjugate Update

\begin{columns}[t,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Stage 1: Global update}
\small

The global subset defines an intermediate Beta distribution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_G(\vx)
&=
\alpha_0(\vx)
+
\sum_{i\in G}
k_g(\vx,\vx_i;\vtheta_g)y_i,\\[0.4em]
\beta_G(\vx)
&=
\beta_0(\vx)
+
\sum_{i\in G}
k_g(\vx,\vx_i;\vtheta_g)(m_i-y_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.49\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Stage 2: Local refinement}
\footnotesize

The intermediate distribution is updated using the local neighbours:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_T(\vx)
&=
\alpha_G(\vx)
+
\sum_{i\in L(\vx)}
k_{\ell}(\vx,\vx_i;\theta_{\ell})y_i,\\[0.4em]
\beta_T(\vx)
&=
\beta_G(\vx)
+
\sum_{i\in L(\vx)}
k_{\ell}(\vx,\vx_i;\theta_{\ell})(m_i-y_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{alertblock}{TwinBKP posterior}
\centering
\vspace*{-0.5em}
$$
\pi(\vx)\mid\mathcal D_{I(\vx)}
\sim
\operatorname{Beta}
\left\{
\alpha_T(\vx),
\beta_T(\vx)
\right\},
\qquad
I(\vx)=G\cup L(\vx).
$$
\end{alertblock}


### Kernel Tuning and Computational Scaling

\begin{block}{Default subset sizes}
\small

$$
g=
\min\left\{
n-1,\,
50d,\,
\max\left(\lfloor\sqrt{n}\rfloor,10d\right)
\right\},
\qquad
\ell=
\min\left\{
n-g,\,
\max(25,3d)
\right\}.
$$

\end{block}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{columns}[T,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.49\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Kernel parameters}
\footnotesize
\begin{itemize}
\item The global length scale \(\vtheta_g\) is selected by the same LOOCV criterion as full BKP, but using only the global subset.

\item The local range parameter is not separately optimized. It is set to the empirical covering radius
$$
\widehat{\theta}_{\ell}
=
\max_{1\le i\le n}
\min_{j\in G}
\|\vx_i-\vx_j\|_2
$$
on the normalized input scale.
\end{itemize}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Computational cost}
\footnotesize
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fitting:}\quad
&\mathcal{O}(n\log n+T_g g^2),\\[0.4em]
\text{Memory:}\quad
&\mathcal{O}(n+g^2),\\[0.4em]
\text{Prediction at \(t\) locations:}\quad
&\mathcal{O}\{t(\log n+g+\ell)\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

No dense \(n\times n\) kernel matrix is stored by default.

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


### Large-$n$ Illustration: TwinBKP

```{r example8-twinbkp, eval=FALSE}
TwinBKP_model <- fit_TwinBKP(X, y, m, Xbounds = Xbounds)
```

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.34\textwidth}

\small

The nonlinear probability function from Example 2 is reused, with the
sample size increased from \(30\) to \(500\).

\begin{block}{Realized default structure}
\footnotesize
\begin{center}
$g=22$

$\ell=25$

Global kernel: Gaussian

Local kernel: Wendland
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.62\textwidth}

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex8.pdf}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
\href{https://jiangyan-zhao.github.io/BKP-website/?section=examples}
{\textcolor{blue}{Explore the interactive TwinBKP example}}
\end{center}


### TwinBKP Prediction for Mourning Warbler

\begin{center}
\hspace*{-0.1\textwidth}
\includegraphics[width=1.2\linewidth]{mourning_warbler_map_twinbkp.pdf}
\end{center}


### Mourning Warbler: BKP versus LGP

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mourning_warbler_map_lgp.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\small
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Model & AUC & Brier score & Elapsed time (s) \\
\midrule
BKP     & 0.888 & 0.0691 & 8.51 \\
TwinBKP & 0.912 & 0.0669 & 0.05 \\
LGP \citep{golding2016fast}     & 0.852 & 0.0798 & 535.50 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}


## DKP

### Extension to Multiclass Responses: DKP

\begin{alertblock}{Key idea}
\[
\begin{array}{ccc}
\text{Binomial response}
& \longrightarrow &
\text{Multinomial response}
\\[0.4em]
\text{Beta distribution}
& \longrightarrow &
\text{Dirichlet distribution}
\\[0.4em]
\pi(\vx)
& \longrightarrow &
\boldsymbol{\pi}(\vx)
=
\{\pi_1(\vx),\ldots,\pi_q(\vx)\}
\end{array}
\]
\end{alertblock}

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{block}{Kernel-weighted Dirichlet update}
\small

Let \(\mathbf{Y}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times q}\) contain the observed class counts.
Then
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}(\vx)\mid\mathcal D_n
\sim
\operatorname{Dirichlet}
\left\{
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_n(\vx)
\right\},
$$
$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_n(\vx)
=
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_0(\vx)
+
\vk(\vx)^\top\mathbf{Y}.
$$

\end{block}


\begin{center}
\small
DKP extends the BKP update from a scalar success probability to an input-dependent vector of class probabilities.
\end{center}


### Multiclass Illustration: Iris Classification

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex7.pdf}

\vspace*{-1em}

\begin{center}
\href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper/blob/master/code/s4_ex7_dkp_iris_classification.R}{\textcolor{blue}{Reproduction code}}
\end{center}


# Summary

### Summary

\begin{alertblock}{One Grammar, Four Models}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
Response type
& Full-data model
& Global--local approximation \\
\midrule

Binary or binomial
& \fct{fit\_BKP}
& \fct{fit\_TwinBKP} \\[0.3em]

Categorical or multinomial
& \fct{fit\_DKP}
&\fct{fit\_TwinDKP} \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{alertblock}

\begin{block}{Computational Profile}

\small

\centering
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}

\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Method
& Inference
& Fitting / tuning
& Memory
& Pointwise prediction \\
\midrule

BKP
&
\multirow{2}{*}{\shortstack{Direct,\\closed-form}}
&
\(\mathcal{O}(Tn^2)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n^2)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n)\)
\\

TwinBKP
&
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n\log n+T_g g^2)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n+g^2)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(\log n+g+\ell)\)
\\

Dense LGP
&
\shortstack{Latent,\\approximate}
&
\(\mathcal{O}(T_{\mathrm L}n^3)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n^2)\)
&
\(\mathcal{O}(n^2)\)
\\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\end{block}


### Future Research Directions

\begin{columns}[T,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Theory}
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item Consistency and convergence rates for full BKP and DKP

\item Approximation-error bounds for the Twin variants

\end{itemize}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Calibration}
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item Finite-sample 95\% marginal coverage for future responses

\item Conditional and simultaneous calibration across the input space

\end{itemize}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.35em}

\begin{block}{Broader modeling scope}
\small

\begin{columns}[T,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}
\item Longitudinal, spatio-temporal, functional,
and multivariate data
\end{itemize}
\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}
\item Over-dispersed counts and other
non-binomial outcome families
\end{itemize}
\end{column}

\end{columns}

\end{block}


### Resources

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.51\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Software}
\small

\textcolor{blue}{\faRProject}\quad
\href{https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/BKP/BKP.html}
{\textcolor{blue}{BKP on CRAN: package and documentation}}

\vspace{0.65em}

\textcolor{black}{\faGithub}\quad
\href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP}
{{\textcolor{blue}{Source code and package development}}}

\end{block}

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{block}{Paper and project materials}
\small

\textcolor{red}{\faFilePdf}\quad
\href{https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10447}
{\textcolor{blue}{BKP software paper on arXiv}}

\vspace{0.65em}

\textcolor{black}{\faGithub}\quad
\href{https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP-paper}
{\textcolor{blue}{Code, data, results, figures, slides, and manuscript}}

\end{block}

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{block}{Project website}
\small

\href{https://jiangyan-zhao.github.io/BKP-website/}
{\textcolor{blue}{Interactive examples, installation guidance, and resources}}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.43\textwidth}

\centering

\includegraphics[width=0.90\linewidth]{qr_website.png}

\vspace{0.25em}

\small
\textbf{Scan to visit the BKP website}

\vspace{0.15em}

\footnotesize
\href{https://jiangyan-zhao.github.io/BKP-website/}
{\textcolor{blue}{jiangyan-zhao.github.io/BKP-website}}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


<!-- --- -->

<!-- ```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='60%'} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics("Thanks.png") -->
<!-- ``` -->
